grammarLessons.name: -(으)ㄴ/는 것 같아요 — It Seems / I Think

cards.grammarNote:
**행복하다** = to be happy (deep, lasting happiness) → **행복해요**.
**기쁘다** = to be glad/delighted (in the moment) → **기뻐요**.
Korean emotions are adjectives that become predicates directly.

**슬픈** (seulpeun) is the adjective modifier form of **슬프다** (seulpeuda, to be sad).
**슬프다** → **슬퍼요** (seulpeoyo) in polite speech — irregular ㅡ drop before vowel endings.
Related noun: **슬픔** (seulpeum) = sadness.
Use **슬퍼요** as a predicate: **왜 슬퍼요?** = Why are you sad?

**사랑** (sarang) is a pure Korean noun meaning love.
The verb form is **사랑하다** (saranghada) → **사랑해요** (saranghaeyo) in polite speech.
**사랑을 느껴요** = I feel love — **을** (eul) is the object particle after a consonant-final noun.
Contrast **사랑** (romantic/deep love) with **좋아함** (joaham, liking/fondness).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel a lot of love for my family.

cards.grammarNote:
**화** (hwa) = anger/fire; a short pure Korean noun.
The idiom **화가 나다** (hwa-ga nada) literally means 'anger rises/comes out' — **가** is the subject particle.
**화가 났어요** = I got angry (past tense: **나다** → **났어요**).
Stronger synonym: **분노** (bunno) — Sino-Korean for wrath/fury.
cards.englishPhrase: I felt anger when they lied to me.

cards.grammarNote:
**두려움** (duryeoum) is the noun form of **두렵다** (duryeopda, to be fearful) — the ㅂ irregular: ㅂ → 워 before vowels.
**두렵다** → **두려워요** (duryeowoyo) in polite speech.
Related: **무섭다** (museopda) = to be scary/scared → **무서워요** (museowoyo).
**두려움** vs **공포** (gongpo): **두려움** is personal dread; **공포** (Sino-Korean) is terror/horror.

**희망** (huimang) is Sino-Korean: 希望 — hope/wish.
As a verb: **희망하다** (huimanghada) → **희망해요**; in everyday speech **바라다** (barada) is more natural.
**희망이 있어요** = there is hope — **이** (i) is the subject particle after consonant **망**.
Contrast **희망** (long-term hope) with **기대** (gidae, expectation/anticipation).
cards.englishPhrase: I have hope for the future.

cards.grammarNote:
**스트레스** (seuteurese) is a loanword from English 'stress'.
Common phrase: **스트레스를 받다** (seuteurese-reul batda) = to receive/get stress.
**스트레스가 많아요** = I have a lot of stress — **가** is the subject particle; **많다** (manta) = to be many/much.
Related: **스트레스를 풀다** (seuteurese-reul pulda) = to relieve stress.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of stress at work.

cards.grammarNote:
**기분** (gibun) is Sino-Korean: 氣分 — lit. 'energy-portion'; mood/feeling.
**기분이 좋아요** = I'm in a good mood (lit. 'my mood is good') — **이** is the subject particle.
Korean describes mood states as noun + adjective rather than verb: **기분이 나빠요** = I'm in a bad mood.
Related: **기분 전환** (gibun jeonhwan) = mood change/a change of pace.
cards.englishPhrase: I'm in a good mood today.

cards.grammarNote:
**감정** (gamjeong) is Sino-Korean: 感情 — emotion/feeling (the full range of emotions).
Distinct from **기분** (gibun, momentary mood) — **감정** refers to deeper emotional states.
**감정이에요** = it is an emotion — **이에요** is the copula after a consonant-final noun.
Related: **감정적** (gamjeongjeok) = emotional (adjective); **감정을 숨기다** = to hide one's feelings.

**감동** (gamdong) = being moved/touched emotionally — Sino-Korean 感動.
Note the phrase uses **감동** (deep emotional impact) rather than plain **감정** (emotion).
**숨길 수 없었어요** = could not hide — **-ㄹ/을 수 없다** means 'cannot' + past tense **-었어요**.
**감동받다** (gamdong batda) = to be moved/touched; **감동시키다** = to move someone emotionally.

**침착함** (chimchakam) is the nominalized form of **침착하다** (chimchakada, to be composed/calm).
Nominalization: **침착하다** + **-ㅁ** → **침착함** (noun: composure).
**침착함을 유지하다** = to maintain composure — **을** is the object particle; **유지하다** = to maintain.
Related: **침착하게** (chimchakage) = calmly (adverb form).

**평화** (pyeonghwa) is Sino-Korean: 平和 — peace/harmony.
Adjective form: **평화롭다** (pyeonghwaropda) → **평화로워요** (pyeonghwarowoyo) — ㅂ irregular.
**고요함** (goyoham) = quietness/stillness, nominalized from **고요하다** (goyohada).
**와/과** (wa/gwa) = and/with — **평화와** uses **와** after the vowel-final **화**.

**걱정** (geokjeong) = worry/concern — pure Korean noun.
Verb form: **걱정하다** (geokjeonghada) → **걱정해요** in polite speech.
**걱정 때문에** = because of worry — **때문에** (ttaemune) expresses cause/reason.
**걱정하지 마세요** = please don't worry — **-지 마세요** is the polite negative imperative.

**놀라움** (nollaum) is the noun form of **놀랍다** (nollapda, to be surprising) — ㅂ irregular.
**놀랍다** → **놀라워요** (nollawoyo) in polite speech (ㅂ → 워 before vowels).
The verb **놀라다** (nollada) = to be startled/surprised → **놀랐어요** (past: I was surprised).
Contrast: **놀람** (nollam) = sudden shock; **놀라움** = a sense of wonder/amazement.

**열정** (yeoljeong) is Sino-Korean: 熱情 — lit. 'hot emotion'; passion/zeal.
Used as: **열정을 가지다** = to have passion; **열정적** (yeoljeongjeok) = passionate (adjective).
**가지고** (gajigo) = holding/having — connective form of **가지다** (to have/hold).
Related: **열의** (yeorui) = enthusiasm; **열심히** (yeolsimhi) = diligently/passionately.

**욕망** (yokmang) is Sino-Korean: 欲望 — desire/craving (often intense or base desires).
**욕구** (yokgu, 欲求) is a milder synonym meaning drive/need — used in the example phrase.
**-고 싶다** (go sipda) = want to (do verb) — e.g. **여행하고 싶은** = wanting to travel.
**강한** (ganghan) = strong — adjective modifying **욕구**; from **강하다** (ganghada).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a strong desire to travel.

cards.grammarNote:
**증오** (jeungo) is Sino-Korean: 憎惡 — intense hatred/loathing.
Verb form: **증오하다** (jeungohada) → **증오해요**.
Milder everyday word: **싫다** (silta) = to dislike → **싫어요** (sireoyo).
**감정이에요** = it is an emotion — **이에요** is the polite copula after consonant-final nouns.

**기쁨** (gippeum) is the noun form of **기쁘다** (gippeuda, to be glad/joyful) — ㅡ irregular.
**기쁘다** → **기뻐요** (gippeoyo) in polite speech (ㅡ drops before 어).
**반갑습니다** (bangapseumnida) = Pleased to meet you — formal -습니다 ending from **반갑다** (to be glad to see).
Related: **즐거움** (jeulgeoum) = enjoyment; **행복** (haengbok) = deep happiness.
cards.englishPhrase: It's a pleasure to meet you.

cards.grammarNote:
**고통** (gotong) is Sino-Korean: 苦痛 — suffering/anguish (emotional or physical).
Physical pain is also **통증** (tongjeung, 痛症) — specifically a medical ache.
**느껴져요** = is felt — passive form of **느끼다** using **-아/어지다** construction.
Related: **고통스럽다** (gotongseureupda) = to be painful/agonizing → **고통스러워요** (ㅂ irregular).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel pain in my leg.

cards.grammarNote:
**에너지** (eneoji) is a loanword from English 'energy'.
Native Korean equivalent: **기운** (gieun) = vitality/vigor/energy.
**에너지가 넘쳐요** = energy overflows — **넘치다** (neomchida) = to overflow/be full of.
**가** is the subject particle after the vowel-final **에너지**.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of energy today.

cards.grammarNote:
**안도** (ando) is Sino-Korean: 安堵 — relief/reassurance.
The phrase uses **다행** (dahaeng) instead — meaning 'fortunate/lucky', more colloquial for relief.
**다행이에요** = it's a relief/I'm glad — **이에요** is the copula after consonant-final **행**.
Related: **안심하다** (ansimhada) = to feel at ease; **안도하다** = to feel relieved.

**위로** (wiro) is Sino-Korean: 慰勞 — consolation/comfort.
Verb form: **위로하다** (wirohada) → **위로해요**.
**위로가 됐어요** = became a comfort — **되다** (doeda) = to become; past tense **됐어요**.
**의** (ui) is the possessive particle: **그녀의 말** = her words.

**느끼다** = to feel/sense something.
**기분이 좋아요** = I feel good (lit. 'my mood is good').
Korean often expresses emotional states as noun + adjective: **기분** (mood/feeling) + state adjective, rather than using a verb.

**사랑하다** (saranghada) = to love — **사랑** (noun) + **하다** (to do) compound verb.
**사랑하다** → **사랑해요** (saranghaeyo) in polite speech — **하다** → **해요**.
**당신** (dangsin) = you (formal/written); in speech, names or **너** (neo, informal) are more natural.
**많이** (manhi) = a lot/much — adverb from **많다** (to be many).

**싫어하다** (sireohada) = to hate/dislike — from **싫다** (silta, to be unpleasant) + **-어하다**.
**싫어하다** → **싫어해요** (sireohaeyo) in polite speech.
**기다리는 걸** = waiting [object] — **-는** nominalizes the verb; **걸** = **것을** (thing/act + obj particle).
Distinct from **증오하다** (jeungohada): **싫어하다** = general dislike; **증오하다** = deep hatred.

**좋아하다** (joahada) = to like — from the adjective **좋다** (jota, to be good) + **-아하다**.
**좋아하다** → **좋아해요** (joahaeyo) in polite speech.
Contrast **좋다** (jota, it is good/I like it — stative) with **좋아하다** (active feeling of liking).
**음악을 좋아해요** = I like music — **을** is the object particle after consonant-final **악**.

**즐기다** (jeulgida) = to enjoy — pure Korean verb.
**즐기다** → **즐겨요** (jeulgyeoyo) in polite speech — ㅣ stem drops before 어: **기** → **겨**.
Related: **즐거운** (jeulgeoun) = fun/enjoyable (adjective); **즐거움** (jeulgeoum) = enjoyment (noun).
**주말을 즐겨요** = I enjoy the weekend — **을** is the object particle.

**두려워하다** (duryeowohada) = to fear — **두렵다** (adjective) + **-어하다** → action verb.
**두려워하다** → **두려워해요** (duryeowohaeyo) in polite speech.
The ㅂ irregular: **두렵다** → **두려워** before vowels — ㅂ changes to 워.
**최악의 상황** = the worst situation — **최악** (choerak, 最惡) is Sino-Korean for 'worst'.

**바라다** (barada) = to wish/hope — pure Korean verb, more heartfelt than **희망하다**.
**바라다** → **바라요** (barayo) in polite speech — vowel stem, so -아요 is added.
**되길 바라요** = I hope it goes well — **-길** is a shortening of **-기를** (nominalizer + object particle).
Related pattern: **-기를 바라다** = to hope that... (formal written form).

**걱정하다** (geokjeonghada) = to worry — **걱정** (noun) + **하다** (to do).
**걱정하다** → **걱정해요** in polite speech; **걱정하지 마세요** = please don't worry.
**그것에 대해** = about that — **에 대해** (e daehae) is a fixed phrase meaning 'regarding/about'.
**마세요** = please don't — the polite negative imperative form, from **말다** (malda, to stop/refrain).

**진정시키다** (jinjeongssikida) = to calm someone down — **진정** + **시키다** (causative: to make/cause).
**진정하다** (jinjeong hada) = to calm oneself down; **진정시키다** = to calm another person.
**진정하세요** = Please calm down — **하세요** is the polite imperative of **하다**.
Related: **진정제** (jinjeongje) = a sedative/tranquilizer; **제** (je) = agent suffix in Sino-Korean.

**위로하다** (wirohada) = to comfort/console — **위로** (Sino-Korean noun) + **하다**.
**위로하다** → **위로해요** (wirohaeyo) in polite speech; past: **위로했어요** (wirohaesseoyo).
**친구를** = friend [object] — **를** is the object particle after vowel-final **구**.
Related: **위안** (wian) = solace/comfort (a more formal Sino-Korean synonym).

**흥분시키다** (heungbunssikida) = to excite — **흥분** (heungbun, excitement) + **시키다** (causative).
The phrase uses **설레다** (seolleda) instead — meaning heart-fluttering anticipatory excitement.
**설레요** (seolleoyo) captures a lighter, romantic or eager excitement vs. intense **흥분**.
**생각하면** = when thinking of — **-(으)면** is the conditional/temporal connector meaning 'if/when'.

**짜증** (jjajeung) = annoyance/irritation — pure Korean noun.
**짜증이 나다** (jjajeungi nada) = annoyance arises; **짜증나게 하다** = to make [something] annoying.
In speech, **짜증 나요** (jjajeung nayo) is most natural: lit. 'annoyance comes out'.
**소음** (soeum, 騷音) = noise — Sino-Korean: **소** (noise) + **음** (sound).

**창피함** (changpiham) is the noun form of **창피하다** (changpihada, to be embarrassed/ashamed).
Nominalization: adjective stem + **-ㅁ** → **창피함**.
**창피해요** (changpihaeyo) = I'm embarrassed — **하다** verb → **해요** in polite speech.
Synonym: **부끄럽다** (bukkeureopda) = to feel shy/ashamed → **부끄러워요** (ㅂ irregular).

**질투** (jiltu) is Sino-Korean: 嫉妬 — jealousy/envy.
Verb form: **질투하다** (jiltuhada) → **질투해요**; **질투나다** = to feel jealous (jealousy arises).
Related: **부러움** (bureouм) = envy (wanting what another has); **질투** focuses on rivalry.
**는** (neun) after **질투** is the topic particle — used here to make a general statement.

**짜증나게 하다** (jjajeungnage hada) = to irritate — **짜증나게** is the adverb form of **짜증나다**.
**-게 하다** is a causative pattern: adjective/verb + **게 하다** = to make [someone/something] do/be.
**기다리는 게** = the act of waiting [subject] — **-는 것이** contracted to **-는 게**.
**짜증 나요** is the natural colloquial form; **짜증나게 하다** is more formal/grammatical.

**화나게 하다** (hwanage hada) = to anger — **화나다** (to become angry) + **-게 하다** (causative).
**화가 나다** = anger arises (intransitive); **화나게 하다** = to cause anger in someone (transitive).
**했어요** is the polite past tense of **하다**: **하다** → **했어요**.
**말** (mal) = words/speech — particle **이** (subject) follows consonant-final **말**.

**기쁘게 하다** (gippeuge hada) = to delight/make happy — **기쁘다** (to be joyful) + **-게 하다**.
**기쁘게** is the adverb form: adjective stem **기쁘** + **-게** → joyfully/in a delightful way.
**기쁘다** has the ㅡ irregular: stem **기쁘** + 어 → **기뻐** (the ㅡ drops before 어).
**모두를** = everyone [object] — **모두** (modu) = all/everyone; **를** after vowel-final **두**.

**미소** (miso) = smile — Sino-Korean: 微笑 (slight smile).
**미소 짓다** (miso jitda) = to smile — lit. 'to make/form a smile'; **짓다** = to make/build.
**미소 지어요** (miso jieoyo) is the polite present — **짓다** has the ㅅ irregular: ㅅ drops before vowels.
Related: **웃다** (utda) = to laugh/smile broadly; **미소** is specifically a gentle smile.

**웃다** (utda) = to laugh/smile — pure Korean verb.
**웃다** → **웃어요** (useoyo) in polite speech — the ㅅ irregular: ㅅ drops before vowel endings.
Past tense: **웃었어요** (uteosseoyo) = laughed.
Related: **웃음** (useum) = laughter/smile (noun); **웃기다** (utgida) = to be funny.

**울다** (ulda) = to cry/weep — pure Korean verb with ㄹ stem.
**울다** → **울어요** (ureoyo) in polite present; **ㄹ** drops before certain endings but stays before 어.
Progressive: **울고 있어요** = is crying — **-고 있다** expresses ongoing action.
Related: **울음** (ureum) = crying (noun); **눈물** (nunmul) = tears (lit. eye-water).

**한숨** (hansum) = sigh — compound: **한** (one/big) + **숨** (breath).
**한숨을 쉬다** = to heave a sigh — **쉬다** (swida) = to breathe/rest; **을** is the object particle.
**안도의 한숨** = a sigh of relief — **의** (ui) is the possessive/genitive particle.
Related: **한숨 짓다** = to sigh (slightly more literary expression).

**찡그리다** (jjinggeurida) = to frown/scrunch up one's face — pure Korean verb.
**인상을 찡그리다** = to frown — **인상** (insang) = facial expression/impression.
**들었을 때** = when [he] heard — **-었을 때** is past tense + **때** (time/when).
Related: **인상을 쓰다** (insangeul sseuda) = to make a sour/frowning face (alternative expression).
cards.englishPhrase: He frowned when he heard the news.
cards.phrase: 그는 소식을 들었을 때 인상을 찡그렸어요.
cards.phraseRomanized: geuneun sosig-eul deureosseul ttae insang-eul jjingeuryeosseoyo

cards.grammarNote:
**소리치다** (sorichida) = to shout/scream — compound: **소리** (sori, sound/voice) + **치다** (to strike/do).
**소리치지 마세요** = please don't shout — **-지 마세요** is the polite negative imperative.
Related: **소리 지르다** (sori jireuda) = to scream loudly; **외치다** (oechida) = to cry out/exclaim.
**소리** alone = sound/noise; **목소리** (moksori) = voice (lit. throat-sound).

**쉬다** (swida) = to rest/relax/breathe — pure Korean verb.
The phrase uses **긴장을 풀다** instead — lit. 'to undo/release tension' — a more natural expression.
**긴장** (ginжang, 緊張) = tension — Sino-Korean; **풀다** (pulda) = to release/solve/undo.
**급하지 않아요** = it's not urgent — **급하다** (geupada) = to be urgent/hurried; **-지 않아요** = negation.

**화가 난** (hwa-ga nan) = angry — lit. 'anger has risen'; noun modifier form of **화가 나다**.
**화가 나다** → past modifier **화가 난** (anger-has-risen), used before nouns.
As predicate: **화가 났어요** = I got/am angry — **났어요** is past tense of **나다** (to arise).
**에게** (ege) = to/toward a person: **당신에게 화가 났어요** = I'm angry at you.

**매우 화난** (maeu hwanan) = very angry — **매우** (maeu) = very; **화난** = angry (adj. form).
**화난** contracts from **화가 난** — often written/spoken as **화난** in compound adjectives.
**그렇게** (geureoke) = so/that much — used to intensify: **왜 그렇게 화가 났어요?**
Synonyms: **몹시 화가 난** (mopsi hwa-ga nan) = extremely angry; **격분한** (gyeokbunhan) = furious.

**무서운** (museoun) is the adjective modifier form of **무섭다** (museopda, to be scary/scared).
**무섭다** → **무서워요** (museowoyo) in polite speech — ㅂ irregular: ㅂ → 워 before vowels.
For expressing personal fear: **무섭다** = it is scary (to me); **두렵다** = I dread/fear it.
**무서워했어요** = was scared — **-어하다** turns an adjective into an active emotional verb (past tense).

**불안한** (buranhan) is the adjective modifier of **불안하다** (buranhada, to be anxious/uneasy).
**불안** (buran) is Sino-Korean: 不安 — lit. 'not-safe'; unease/anxiety.
**불안해요** (buranhaeyo) = I'm anxious — **하다** adjective → **해요** in polite speech.
Related: **불안감** (burangam) = a feeling of anxiety; **불안정** (buranjeong) = instability.

**긴장된** (ginжangdoen) = nervous/tensed — **긴장** (tension) + **된** (become, past passive modifier).
**긴장하다** (ginжanghada) = to become tense/nervous; **긴장돼요** = I'm nervous (contraction of **되어요**).
**긴장** (緊張) is Sino-Korean — lit. 'tight-stretch'; commonly used for pre-event nerves.
Contrast with **불안하다** (anxiety) — **긴장하다** is often situational/performance nerves.

**신이 난** (sini nan) = excited — lit. 'spirit/energy has risen'; **신** (spirit/enthusiasm) + **이 나다**.
The phrase uses **기대돼요** = I'm looking forward to it — **기대** (gidae, 期待) = expectation.
**기대되다** = to be anticipated — **되다** suffix makes a passive/spontaneous feeling verb.
Contrast: **신이 나다** (energetic excitement) vs **설레다** (fluttery/romantic anticipation).

**즐거운** (jeulgoun) is the adjective modifier of **즐겁다** (jeulgeopda, to be fun/joyful) — ㅂ irregular.
**즐겁다** → **즐거워요** (jeulgeoweoyo) in polite speech (ㅂ → 워 before vowels).
The phrase uses **명랑하다** (myeongnanghada) — more specifically cheerful/bright in personality.
**항상** (hangsang) = always — adverb used with habitual present tense.

**자랑스러운** is the modifier form of **자랑스럽다** (jarangseureupda, to be proud) — ㅂ irregular.
**자랑스럽다** → **자랑스러워요** (jarangseureoweoyo) in polite speech.
**자랑** (jarang) = boast/pride (noun); **-스럽다** suffix = -ful/-ish (creates descriptive adjective).
**당신이 자랑스러워요** = I'm proud of you — the person you're proud of takes **이/가** (subject particle).

**만족한** is the modifier of **만족하다** (manjokada, to be satisfied/content) — Sino-Korean 滿足.
**만족하다** → **만족해요** (manjokaeyo) in polite speech.
**에 만족하다** = to be satisfied with — **에** (e) marks the thing one is content with.
Related: **만족감** (manjokgam) = a sense of satisfaction; **불만족** (bulmanjok) = dissatisfaction.

**침착한** (chimchakhan) is the modifier form of **침착하다** (chimchakada, to be composed/calm).
**침착하세요** = Please stay calm — **-하세요** is the polite imperative of **하다** adjectives.
**괜찮아요** (gwaenchanhayo) = it's fine/okay — a very common reassurance phrase.
**다** (da) = all/everything — short adverb meaning 'completely' or 'all is'.

**스트레스받는** = stress-receiving — compound modifier: **스트레스** + **받다** (batda, to receive) + **-는**.
**스트레스를 받다** = to be stressed/get stressed (lit. 'to receive stress').
The phrase uses **스트레스가 많아요** = there is a lot of stress — subject + **많다** (to be much).
**이번 주** (ibeon ju) = this week — **이번** (this time/this) + **주** (week, Sino-Korean 週).

**압도된** (apdo-doen) = overwhelmed — **압도** (apdo, 壓倒, to overwhelm) + **된** (passive past modifier).
**압도되다** (apdo-doeda) = to be overwhelmed — **되다** makes it passive/spontaneous.
**느낌이에요** = it's a feeling — **느낌** (neukkum, feeling/sense) + copula **이에요**.
Related: **벅차다** (beokchada) = to feel overwhelmed/welling up — a more emotional pure Korean word.
cards.englishPhrase: I feel overwhelmed by the work.

cards.grammarNote:
**활기찬** (hwalgichan) = energetic/lively — compound: **활기** (hwalgi, vitality) + **차다** (to be full of).
**활기** is Sino-Korean: 活氣 — lit. 'living-energy'; vibrancy/liveliness.
**활기차다** → **활기차요** (hwalgichayo) in polite speech.
Related: **활발하다** (hwalbalada) = to be active/lively; **생기** (saenggi) = vitality/freshness.

**피곤한** (pigonhan) is the modifier form of **피곤하다** (pigonhada, to be tired) — Sino-Korean 疲困.
**피곤하다** → **피곤해요** (pigonhaeyo) in polite speech.
Related pure Korean word: **지치다** (jichida) = to be worn out/exhausted → **지쳐요** (jichyeoyo).
**너무** (neomu) = too/very — intensifier: **너무 피곤해요** = I'm very tired.

**지친** (jichin) is the past modifier of **지치다** (jichida, to be exhausted/worn out) — pure Korean.
**지치다** → **지쳐요** (jichyeoyo) in polite present; **지쳤어요** (jichyeosseoyo) = (I am) exhausted (past).
**완전히** (wanjeonhi) = completely/totally — adverb from **완전하다** (wanjeonhada, to be complete).
Related: **기진맥진하다** (gijinmaekjinhada) = to be utterly exhausted (more emphatic/formal expression).

**궁금한** (gunggeumhan) is the modifier of **궁금하다** (gunggeumhada, to be curious/wondering).
**궁금하다** → **궁금해요** (gunggeumhaeyo) in polite speech.
**궁금하다** is used when you are wondering about something specific: **그게 궁금해요** = I'm curious about that.
Related: **호기심** (hogisim, 好奇心) = curiosity (the feeling itself) — Sino-Korean noun.
